title: Career Strategy
text: title: “Personal Career Path Decisions in 2024” author: “Manjari Kannan, Nicholas Decesaris, Oscar Quintero” date: today format: html: bibliography: references.bib csl: csl/econometrica.csl toc: true docx: toc: true —

title: Career Strategy
text:
Objective
This project examines job market trends in 2024 from the perspective of job seekers rather than recruiters. Using Lightcast data, we analyze hiring patterns, salaries, skills, and labor market conditions to understand how students can position themselves more effectively.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Research Question
Our project asks:
What trends in the 2024 job market can help students and early career professionals make better career decisions?

title: Career Strategy
text:
Why This Matters
Rapid changes in AI adoption, salary patterns, remote work, and employer skill expectations make the job market harder to navigate. A data driven approach helps identify which roles are growing, which skills matter most, and what kinds of jobs offer the best opportunities (Dominski and Lee (2025)).

title: Career Strategy
text:
Planned Analysis
This project includes: - data cleaning and preprocessing - exploratory data analysis - unsupervised learning with KMeans clustering - supervised modeling using regression and classification - skill gap analysis - career recommendations for job seekers






ImportantNote from Nakul



Please show the model code and the tables for Ml methods and EDA. I want to see the code and the tables that you generated for those sections.

title: Career Strategy
text: References

title: Career Strategy
text:
Overview
Before conducting exploratory analysis and predictive modeling, the Lightcast job postings dataset must be cleaned and standardized. Because the dataset includes many tracking variables, repeated industry and occupation code versions, and missing values, preprocessing is necessary to improve interpretability and reduce noise.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Goals of Data Cleaning
The main goals of data preparation are to: - remove columns that are irrelevant or redundant - keep the most current classification systems - handle missing values consistently - remove duplicate job postings - prepare the dataset for visualization and machine learning

title: Career Strategy
section: Why Some Columns Should Be Dropped
text:
Why Some Columns Should Be Dropped
The raw dataset contains many columns that are not useful for this project, such as URLs, duplicate tracking fields, and outdated industry and occupation code versions. Keeping too many repeated classification columns can make the analysis harder to interpret and may introduce unnecessary complexity.
For example, older NAICS and SOC versions are less useful when more current classifications are already available in the dataset. To keep the analysis focused and consistent, we retain the latest available fields such as NAICS_2022_6 and SOC_2021_4 and remove redundant older versions.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Example Python Cleaning Code

from pyspark.sql import functions as F

columns_to_drop = [
    "ID",
    "URL",
    "ACTIVE_URLS",
    "DUPLICATES",
    "LAST_UPDATED_TIMESTAMP",
    "NAICS2",
    "NAICS3",
    "NAICS4",
    "NAICS5",
    "NAICS6",
    "SOC_2",
    "SOC_3",
    "SOC_5"
]

df_clean = df.drop(*columns_to_drop)

title: Career Strategy
text:
Handling Missing Values
Missing values are common in labor market data, especially for salary and skill related fields. Different strategies should be used depending on the data type.
For numerical variables such as salary, replacing missing values with the median is more appropriate than using the mean because salary data is often skewed by very high paying jobs. For categorical variables, missing values can be replaced with “Unknown” so that rows are not lost unnecessarily.
Columns with too many missing values may also be removed if they do not provide enough useful information.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Example Missing Value Handling

median_salary = df_clean.approxQuantile("SALARY", [0.5], 0.01)[0]

df_clean = df_clean.fillna({
    "SALARY": median_salary,
    "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME": "Unknown",
    "NAICS_2022_6_NAME": "Unknown",
    "SOC_2021_4_NAME": "Unknown"
})

title: Career Strategy
text:
Removing Duplicate Job Postings
Duplicate job postings can distort the results by counting the same opportunity multiple times. To prevent this, duplicate observations should be removed using a reasonable combination of identifying fields such as title, company, location, and posting date.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Example Duplicate Removal
df_clean = df_clean.dropDuplicates([
    "TITLE",
    "COMPANY_NAME",
    "LOCATION",
    "POSTED"
])

title: Career Strategy
text:
Why This Matters
Data cleaning improves the reliability of every later stage of the project. Cleaner data leads to:

more accurate summaries and charts
better model performance
clearer interpretation for job seekers
more trustworthy career recommendations

title: Career Strategy
text:
Output of Cleaning Step
After cleaning, the dataset is more suitable for:

exploratory analysis of salary, industry, and remote work patterns
clustering by occupation or industry categories
regression models predicting salary
classification models predicting job categories or remote work type

title: Career Strategy
text:
Overview
Before building any models, we explored the Lightcast job postings dataset to understand the underlying patterns in salary, industry composition, and AI-related job demand. This section summarizes what the data revealed about how AI roles compare to non-AI roles across key dimensions.
All analysis is based on the cleaned dataset of 70,892 job postings.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Defining AI vs Non-AI Roles
To distinguish AI-related jobs from the rest of the dataset, we flagged any posting where the job title or skills field contained keywords such as “ai”, “machine learning”, “artificial intelligence”, “deep learning”, “data science”, “nlp”, “llm”, or “neural network”.
This produced 31,857 AI-related postings (approximately 45% of the dataset) and 39,035 non-AI postings (approximately 55%). The near-even split suggests that AI-related roles have moved well beyond a niche segment of the labor market.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Salary Distribution: AI vs Non-AI

The first question we investigated was whether AI roles simply pay more. The overall pattern suggests they do not, at least not uniformly.
Both distributions peak near $112,500 and follow a similar right-skewed shape. The two groups overlap substantially, which indicates that holding an AI-related title does not automatically translate into higher compensation. The relationship between AI and salary appears to be more complex than a simple premium.

title: Career Strategy
section: Average Salary by Industry: AI vs Non-AI
text:
Average Salary by Industry: AI vs Non-AI

Breaking salary down by industry reveals a more nuanced picture. Certain industries show a meaningful AI salary premium, including Arts and Entertainment (approximately +$13,000), Construction (approximately +$13,500), and Retail Trade (approximately +$9,000). In these fields, AI skills remain relatively uncommon, and compensation reflects that scarcity.
In contrast, the Information industry pays AI roles less on average than non-AI roles. Interestingly, “working in an occupation that is considered high skill increases the odds of job loss to AI” (Dahlin2023?)…especially for the “middle-skill employees in the technological landscape” (dahlin2023?). Arguably, the high-skill employes can pivot using tools and higher levels of resources and knowledge to adapt to the shifting environment which does not appear to directly impact salary until it is considered that the majority of the middle-skill employees will must either transition to another field or be entirely displaced. This apparent stability of salary is likely because of the traditional IT and enterprise infrastructure roles in that sector already commanding high salaries, making AI titles less financially distinctive by comparison due to the stability of the high-skill employees mantaining positions.

title: Career Strategy
section: Top Industries by AI Job Posting Count
text:
Top Industries by AI Job Posting Count

In terms of where AI jobs are being posted, Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services leads by a wide margin, followed by Finance and Insurance and Information. While AI-related roles appear across many sectors, the majority of active hiring remains concentrated in knowledge-intensive industries. For job seekers targeting AI roles, these sectors represent the highest volume of opportunity.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Key Findings
Several patterns emerged from this exploratory analysis worth carrying forward into the modeling phase.
The overall salary difference between AI and non-AI roles is negligible at roughly $100 on average. The salary premium associated with AI skills is industry-specific rather than universal. Fields where AI capabilities are still emerging tend to reward those skills more generously. The concentration of AI hiring in professional services, finance, and information suggests that the benefits of AI are not evenly distributed across the workforce. Where a worker is employed matters as much as what skills they bring.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Overview
This section applies three machine learning models to the cleaned Lightcast dataset: a linear regression model to predict salary, a logistic regression model to classify AI versus non-AI roles, and a KMeans clustering model to identify natural salary tiers across the job market. Together these models help quantify how job characteristics relate to compensation and AI demand.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Regression Model: Predicting Salary

Objective
The goal of this model is to predict the midpoint salary of a job posting based on observable job characteristics.


Feature Justification
Two versions of the model were tested. The first used industry, remote work type, and AI role flag as features. The second added occupation category and years of experience. Occupation category was expected to be the strongest predictor because salary varies substantially across professional fields regardless of industry.


Results



Model
Features
RMSE
R2




Model 1
Industry, Remote Type, AI Flag
$31,428
0.0094


Model 2
+ Occupation, Experience
$30,040
0.0949





Interpretation
Adding occupation category and experience improved R2 from 0.009 to 0.095 and reduced RMSE by roughly $1,400. However, the overall explanatory power remains modest. This is expected given that salary is also shaped by factors not present in this dataset, such as company size, specific technical skills, education level, and negotiation. The low R2 is itself a finding worth noting: structured job characteristics explain only a small portion of salary variance, which suggests that individual and firm level factors play a larger role in determining compensation.

title: Career Strategy
section: Classification Model: Predicting AI vs Non-AI Roles
text:
Classification Model: Predicting AI vs Non-AI Roles

Objective
This model attempts to classify whether a job posting is AI-related based on industry, remote work type, and occupation category.


Results



Metric
Score




Accuracy
55.6%


F1 Score
0.48


AUC
0.54





Confusion Matrix



Confusion Matrix


The confusion matrix shows that the model correctly identifies most non-AI roles (6,869 true negatives) but misclassifies the majority of actual AI roles as non-AI (5,426 false negatives). This pattern reflects the core finding that AI roles are not structurally distinguishable from non-AI roles based on industry, remote type, and occupation category alone.


Interpretation
The model performs only slightly better than random guessing. This finding is informative rather than disappointing. It suggests that AI-related roles are spread across industries and occupation categories rather than being concentrated in obvious segments. The distinction between AI and non-AI roles lives primarily in the specific skills and title language of the posting, not in its structural characteristics.

title: Career Strategy
text:
KMeans Clustering: Salary Tiers

Objective
KMeans clustering was used to identify natural groupings in the job market based on industry, occupation, salary, and AI role status. Four clusters were specified based on the expectation that the market would segment into entry, mid, upper mid, and senior tiers.


Results



Cluster
Avg Salary (Non-AI)
Avg Salary (AI)
Job Count




Entry Level
$47,270
$50,670
4,601


Mid Level
$82,528
$82,259
7,700


Upper Mid Level
$114,743
$114,707
51,215


Senior Level
$177,965
$180,191
7,359





Interpretation
The clustering produced four well-separated salary tiers that are intuitive and interpretable. The most striking pattern is that within every cluster, AI and non-AI roles earn nearly identical average salaries. The salary tier a job belongs to is determined by its occupation and industry profile, not by whether it involves AI. This finding is consistent across all three models and forms the central conclusion of this analysis: AI designation alone does not drive salary. Structural job characteristics and market positioning matter more.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Overview
This section examines which skills are most in demand across AI and non-AI job postings in the Lightcast dataset, and compares those findings to the skill profile of a typical business analytics student. The goal is to identify where gaps exist and what students should prioritize to remain competitive in an AI-influenced job market.

title: Career Strategy
text:

The fifteen most frequently requested skills across AI-related job postings are listed below.

title: Career Strategy
section: Comparing AI and Non-AI Skill Demand
text:
Comparing AI and Non-AI Skill Demand
Several patterns emerge when comparing the two lists. Communication, Data Analysis, Management, SQL, and Problem Solving rank highly in both, confirming that these are foundational skills regardless of whether a role is AI-related. However, AI roles place noticeably higher demand on Python, Tableau, Data Science, and Dashboard skills, while non-AI roles emphasize SAP Applications, Business Requirements, Finance, and Business Process knowledge.
This suggests that the transition into AI-related work requires building on a shared analytical foundation while adding more specialized technical and data visualization capabilities.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Skill Gap Assessment
A typical business analytics graduate entering this job market is likely comfortable with communication, Excel, SQL, and basic data analysis. Based on the demand patterns observed in this dataset, the most significant gaps tend to fall in the following areas.
Python programming appears in the top ten for AI roles but not at all in the non-AI top fifteen, making it one of the clearest differentiators for students targeting AI-related positions. Tableau and dashboard development are heavily requested in AI roles and represent a practical visualization skill that complements SQL and Excel. Data Science as a named competency appears frequently in AI postings and signals employer expectations around modeling, statistical reasoning, and analytical workflows beyond basic reporting.
On the non-AI side, familiarity with enterprise platforms such as SAP and business process documentation skills appear prominently, suggesting that students targeting traditional analyst or operations roles should also develop comfort with enterprise systems.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Recommendations
Students aiming for AI-related roles should prioritize Python, Tableau, and applied data science coursework. Those targeting traditional business or operations roles will benefit from developing familiarity with SAP and structured business process skills. In either path, SQL, communication, and analytical thinking remain the most universally valued capabilities in the current labor market.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Overview
This section translates the findings from our exploratory analysis, predictive modeling, and skill gap assessment into actionable career guidance for students and early-career job seekers navigating an AI-influenced labor market.

title: Career Strategy
text:
What the Data Tells Us
Several consistent findings emerged across all stages of this project that should directly inform career planning decisions.
AI-related roles do not universally pay more. Recent studies suggest that “increased AI exposure may be correlated with higher earnings in some contexts, but the strength and direction of this relationship are sensitive to model specification[s] (dominski2025?).” Within the findings of this project, for instance, the average salary difference between AI and non-AI roles in this dataset is approximately $100, which is negligible. Salary is determined more by industry, occupation tier, and job level than by AI designation alone. Students who assume that adding AI skills to their resume will automatically lead to higher pay may be disappointed without also considering the sector and role level they are targeting.
The salary premium for AI skills is industry-specific. Fields such as Arts and Entertainment, Construction, and Retail Trade show AI salary premiums of $9,000 to $13,500, likely because AI capabilities remain scarce in those sectors. In contrast, the Information industry pays non-AI roles more on average because that sector already compensates technical work highly regardless of AI involvement. Job seekers should research salary norms within their target industry rather than relying on general AI salary narratives.
Salary tier matters more than AI status. The KMeans clustering analysis identified four distinct salary tiers in the job market ranging from approximately $49,000 at the entry level to $179,000 at the senior level. Within every tier, AI and non-AI roles earn nearly identical salaries. Moving up a salary tier requires developing the occupation and industry profile associated with that tier, not simply acquiring AI-related keywords.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Recommended Career Paths
Based on the skill demand patterns and salary distributions observed in this dataset, three career directions appear particularly well-suited for business analytics students.
Students with strong SQL, Python, and data visualization skills are well positioned for data science and analytics roles, which appear frequently in the Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services sector and consistently fall in the upper mid salary tier around $114,000.
Students interested in finance and insurance will find that sector offers strong AI role concentration and competitive salaries. Finance-adjacent analytical roles that combine business knowledge with data skills appear to be in high demand.
Students targeting non-AI paths should not overlook the value of enterprise systems experience. SAP, business process management, and ERP skills appear prominently in non-AI postings and are associated with stable demand across multiple industries.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Priority Skills to Develop
Based on the skill gap analysis, the highest-return skills for students to develop before entering the job market are Python programming, SQL, Tableau or equivalent dashboard tools, and applied data science competencies. Communication, problem solving, and project management round out the universal skills that employers expect regardless of role type.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Final Takeaway
The rise of AI is reshaping the job market, but not in the simple way that headlines often suggest. Our analysis shows that AI skills matter most in specific industries and role contexts, that salary outcomes depend heavily on occupation tier and sector, and that foundational analytical skills remain the most consistently valued capabilities across the entire labor market. Students who build a strong analytical foundation and develop targeted technical skills for their chosen industry will be better positioned than those who chase AI credentials without strategic direction.

title: Personal Career Path Decisions in 2024
text:
This project examines job market trends in 2024 from the perspective of job seekers rather than recruiters. Using Lightcast data, we analyze hiring patterns, salaries, skills, and labor market conditions to understand how students can position themselves more effectively.



Our project asks:
What trends in the 2024 job market can help students and early career professionals make better career decisions?



Rapid changes in AI adoption, salary patterns, remote work, and employer skill expectations make the job market harder to navigate. A data driven approach helps identify which roles are growing, which skills matter most, and what kinds of jobs offer the best opportunities (Dominski and Lee (2025)).



This project includes: - data cleaning and preprocessing - exploratory data analysis - unsupervised learning with KMeans clustering - supervised modeling using regression and classification - skill gap analysis - career recommendations for job seekers

title: Personal Career Path Decisions in 2024
text: This project examines job market trends in 2024 from the perspective of job seekers rather than recruiters. Using Lightcast data, we analyze hiring patterns, salaries, skills, and labor market conditions to understand how students can position themselves more effectively.

title: Personal Career Path Decisions in 2024
text:
Our project asks:
What trends in the 2024 job market can help students and early career professionals make better career decisions?

title: Personal Career Path Decisions in 2024
text: Rapid changes in AI adoption, salary patterns, remote work, and employer skill expectations make the job market harder to navigate. A data driven approach helps identify which roles are growing, which skills matter most, and what kinds of jobs offer the best opportunities (Dominski and Lee (2025)).

title: Personal Career Path Decisions in 2024
text:
This project includes: - data cleaning and preprocessing - exploratory data analysis - unsupervised learning with KMeans clustering - supervised modeling using regression and classification - skill gap analysis - career recommendations for job seekers






ImportantNote from Nakul



Please show the model code and the tables for Ml methods and EDA. I want to see the code and the tables that you generated for those sections.

title: Career Strategy
text: This section translates the findings from our exploratory analysis, predictive modeling, and skill gap assessment into actionable career guidance for students and early-career job seekers navigating an AI-influenced labor market.

title: Career Strategy
text: This section translates the findings from our exploratory analysis, predictive modeling, and skill gap assessment into actionable career guidance for students and early-career job seekers navigating an AI-influenced labor market.

title: Career Strategy
text:
What the Data Tells Us
Several consistent findings emerged across all stages of this project that should directly inform career planning decisions.
AI-related roles do not universally pay more. Recent studies suggest that “increased AI exposure may be correlated with higher earnings in some contexts, but the strength and direction of this relationship are sensitive to model specification[s] (dominski2025?).” Within the findings of this project, for instance, the average salary difference between AI and non-AI roles in this dataset is approximately $100, which is negligible. Salary is determined more by industry, occupation tier, and job level than by AI designation alone. Students who assume that adding AI skills to their resume will automatically lead to higher pay may be disappointed without also considering the sector and role level they are targeting.
The salary premium for AI skills is industry-specific. Fields such as Arts and Entertainment, Construction, and Retail Trade show AI salary premiums of $9,000 to $13,500, likely because AI capabilities remain scarce in those sectors. In contrast, the Information industry pays non-AI roles more on average because that sector already compensates technical work highly regardless of AI involvement. Job seekers should research salary norms within their target industry rather than relying on general AI salary narratives.
Salary tier matters more than AI status. The KMeans clustering analysis identified four distinct salary tiers in the job market ranging from approximately $49,000 at the entry level to $179,000 at the senior level. Within every tier, AI and non-AI roles earn nearly identical salaries. Moving up a salary tier requires developing the occupation and industry profile associated with that tier, not simply acquiring AI-related keywords.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Recommended Career Paths
Based on the skill demand patterns and salary distributions observed in this dataset, three career directions appear particularly well-suited for business analytics students.
Students with strong SQL, Python, and data visualization skills are well positioned for data science and analytics roles, which appear frequently in the Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services sector and consistently fall in the upper mid salary tier around $114,000.
Students interested in finance and insurance will find that sector offers strong AI role concentration and competitive salaries. Finance-adjacent analytical roles that combine business knowledge with data skills appear to be in high demand.
Students targeting non-AI paths should not overlook the value of enterprise systems experience. SAP, business process management, and ERP skills appear prominently in non-AI postings and are associated with stable demand across multiple industries.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Priority Skills to Develop
Based on the skill gap analysis, the highest-return skills for students to develop before entering the job market are Python programming, SQL, Tableau or equivalent dashboard tools, and applied data science competencies. Communication, problem solving, and project management round out the universal skills that employers expect regardless of role type.

title: Career Strategy
text:
Final Takeaway
The rise of AI is reshaping the job market, but not in the simple way that headlines often suggest. Our analysis shows that AI skills matter most in specific industries and role contexts, that salary outcomes depend heavily on occupation tier and sector, and that foundational analytical skills remain the most consistently valued capabilities across the entire labor market. Students who build a strong analytical foundation and develop targeted technical skills for their chosen industry will be better positioned than those who chase AI credentials without strategic direction.

title: Job Market Analysis 2024
text:
This project analyzes 2024 labor market trends using Lightcast job posting data. The goal is to understand how salaries, skills, industries, remote work, and predictive modeling can help job seekers make better career decisions.


Our analysis explores:

job market demand across roles and industries
salary and compensation patterns
remote versus on site opportunities
predictive models for salary and job classification
skill gaps between student preparation and market expectations

title: Data Preparation
text: Before conducting exploratory analysis and predictive modeling, the Lightcast job postings dataset must be cleaned and standardized. Because the dataset includes many tracking variables, repeated industry and occupation code versions, and missing values, preprocessing is necessary to improve interpretability and reduce noise.

title: Data Preparation
text: Before conducting exploratory analysis and predictive modeling, the Lightcast job postings dataset must be cleaned and standardized. Because the dataset includes many tracking variables, repeated industry and occupation code versions, and missing values, preprocessing is necessary to improve interpretability and reduce noise.

title: Data Preparation
text:
Goals of Data Cleaning
The main goals of data preparation are to: - remove columns that are irrelevant or redundant - keep the most current classification systems - handle missing values consistently - remove duplicate job postings - prepare the dataset for visualization and machine learning

title: Data Preparation
section: Why Some Columns Should Be Dropped
text:
Why Some Columns Should Be Dropped
The raw dataset contains many columns that are not useful for this project, such as URLs, duplicate tracking fields, and outdated industry and occupation code versions. Keeping too many repeated classification columns can make the analysis harder to interpret and may introduce unnecessary complexity.
For example, older NAICS and SOC versions are less useful when more current classifications are already available in the dataset. To keep the analysis focused and consistent, we retain the latest available fields such as NAICS_2022_6 and SOC_2021_4 and remove redundant older versions.

title: Data Preparation
text:
Example Python Cleaning Code

from pyspark.sql import functions as F

columns_to_drop = [
    "ID",
    "URL",
    "ACTIVE_URLS",
    "DUPLICATES",
    "LAST_UPDATED_TIMESTAMP",
    "NAICS2",
    "NAICS3",
    "NAICS4",
    "NAICS5",
    "NAICS6",
    "SOC_2",
    "SOC_3",
    "SOC_5"
]

df_clean = df.drop(*columns_to_drop)

title: Data Preparation
text:
Handling Missing Values
Missing values are common in labor market data, especially for salary and skill related fields. Different strategies should be used depending on the data type.
For numerical variables such as salary, replacing missing values with the median is more appropriate than using the mean because salary data is often skewed by very high paying jobs. For categorical variables, missing values can be replaced with “Unknown” so that rows are not lost unnecessarily.
Columns with too many missing values may also be removed if they do not provide enough useful information.

title: Data Preparation
text:
Example Missing Value Handling

median_salary = df_clean.approxQuantile("SALARY", [0.5], 0.01)[0]

df_clean = df_clean.fillna({
    "SALARY": median_salary,
    "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME": "Unknown",
    "NAICS_2022_6_NAME": "Unknown",
    "SOC_2021_4_NAME": "Unknown"
})

title: Data Preparation
text:
Removing Duplicate Job Postings
Duplicate job postings can distort the results by counting the same opportunity multiple times. To prevent this, duplicate observations should be removed using a reasonable combination of identifying fields such as title, company, location, and posting date.

title: Data Preparation
text:
Example Duplicate Removal
df_clean = df_clean.dropDuplicates([
    "TITLE",
    "COMPANY_NAME",
    "LOCATION",
    "POSTED"
])

title: Data Preparation
text:
Why This Matters
Data cleaning improves the reliability of every later stage of the project. Cleaner data leads to:

more accurate summaries and charts
better model performance
clearer interpretation for job seekers
more trustworthy career recommendations

title: Data Preparation
text:
Output of Cleaning Step
After cleaning, the dataset is more suitable for:

exploratory analysis of salary, industry, and remote work patterns
clustering by occupation or industry categories
regression models predicting salary
classification models predicting job categories or remote work type
